titel: Law regarding fees for area-based collaboration.
html:
The government proposes a new law regarding fees for area-based collaboration.

With the support of this law, organizations engaged in area-based collaboration may be granted the right to charge a fee from the property owners involved. The organization conducting the collaboration could be, for example, an association of property owners and other stakeholders.

The right to charge a fee under the law, and the corresponding obligation to pay the fee, makes it possible to distribute the costs of the collaboration among all affected property owners in proportion to the benefit they may derive from the collaborative work. The fee can only cover costs for activities that are not covered by the responsibility of the state or the municipality.

To be granted the right to charge a fee, it is required that the collaboration constitutes "area-based collaboration," meaning that various actors, within a geographically defined area of the built environment, collaborate to analyze needs and take actions to develop the area in a way that prevents crime, promotes safety, and enhances the area's attractiveness, local businesses, and ultimately, the common good of the area.

To be granted the right to charge a fee, the organization conducting the collaboration must represent a qualified majority of the affected property owners. There must be a need for area-based collaboration that is not being met by any other means.

To be allowed to charge a fee under the law, the organization conducting the collaboration must be approved as the main operator for the collaborative work. The municipality assesses applications for approval as the main operator.

The municipality may, when there is a significant need for area-based collaboration that is not being met by other means, decide to act as the main operator itself. In all cases regarding the main operator, the municipality must provide the affected property owners with the opportunity to express their opinions. Disputes between the main operator and property owners regarding the fee are handled by the general courts.

According to the proposal, the law will come into force on August 1, 2026.

Proposal 2025/26:157

Government Proposal: The Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) approves the government's proposal for a law regarding fees for area-based collaboration. 3

Proposal 2025/26:157 2 Proposed Law on Fees for Area-Based Collaboration

Hereby is prescribed the following:

Chapter 1. Scope of the Law Section 1. This law grants the right for entities conducting area-based collaboration to charge fees from owners of properties and buildings to cover collaboration costs.

Definitions Section 2. In this law, the following terms have the following meanings:

  • "Area-based collaboration": Collaboration within a geographically defined area in the built environment that analyzes the need for and implements measures to develop the area in a way that contributes to preventing crime, creating safety, and strengthening the area's attractiveness and local business, thereby contributing to the area's overall well-being.
  • "Collaboration area": The geographically defined area covered by an area-based collaboration.
  • "Collaboration property": A property or building within a collaboration area, except for single-family homes or duplexes, accessory buildings to such homes, or a property consisting only of such homes.
  • "Collaboration property owner": The owner of a collaboration property or the owner of a building located on another's collaboration property, except for single-family homes or duplexes or accessory buildings to such homes.
  • "Collaboration plan": An updated operational plan that describes the work and measures planned within the framework of area-based collaboration, both in the short term and long term.
  • "Collaboration cost": A cost that may be covered by a fee according to this law.
  • "Collaboration fee": A fee that may be charged according to this law to cover a collaboration cost.
  • "Benefit factor": A number, according to this law, that indicates the percentage of benefit a collaboration property receives from the activities covered by a collaboration fee.

Section 3. In this law, "collaboration budget" means an updated statement of:

  1. The costs expected to be incurred in implementing the collaboration plan, distributed across calendar years, with information on:

a) Which costs are collaboration costs, and b) Which costs are covered by other legislation or need to be covered in another manner.

  1. How the benefit factors of collaboration properties are determined for the distribution of collaboration fees.
  2. How any surplus or deficit resulting from the difference between collected collaboration fees and collaboration costs is planned to be balanced through refunds or by transferring it to the following calendar year.

Section 4. In this law, "collaboration coordinator" means the entity conducting area-based collaboration and approved by the municipality as the main coordinator for the collaboration work. If the municipality, with the support of this law, has undertaken to conduct area-based collaboration, the municipality is the collaboration coordinator.

Chapter 2. Requirements for Being a Collaboration Coordinator Approval as a Collaboration Coordinator Section 1. Only the following may be approved as a collaboration coordinator:

  1. Entities that demonstrate that area-based collaboration is needed throughout the collaboration area and that this need is not met elsewhere within the area.
  2. Entities that have a collaboration plan and a collaboration budget that make it likely that collaboration fees will be collected according to this law.
  3. Entities that demonstrate that the benefit factors of collaboration properties have been determined according to this law and are reasonable.
  4. Entities that represent at least two-thirds of the collaboration property owners and two-thirds of the benefit factors of the collaboration properties.
  5. Entities that do not conduct the collaboration work for profit.
  6. Entities that provide collaboration property owners who wish to participate in the collaboration work with the opportunity to participate in the decision-making of the activities on reasonable and transparent terms.
  7. Entities that are not, or are not represented by, entities that are insolvent, bankrupt, or likely to fail in their willingness and ability to fulfill their obligations, comply with the law, or have other circumstances that negatively affect their reputation.

Section 2. Applications for approval as a collaboration coordinator are reviewed by the municipality where the collaboration area is located. If the collaboration area is located in more than one municipality, the approval application is reviewed by the municipality where the largest part of the collaboration area is located, or by the municipality that the affected municipalities agree upon. The municipality reviewing the application shall provide the other municipalities with the opportunity to comment before the case is decided.

Section 3. An application for approval as a collaboration coordinator shall include:

  1. The applicant's name, personal or organizational identification number, and address.
  2. A list of the collaboration properties.
  3. A list of the collaboration property owners represented by the applicant.
  4. A list of the collaboration property owners not represented by the applicant.
  5. A description of who participates in the collaboration work and an account of consultations with other relevant stakeholders that are important for the collaboration work, to assess the need for area-based collaboration and what the collaboration should encompass.
  6. The information and documents that the applicant relies on to demonstrate that the conditions in Section 1 are met.

Section 4. Before the municipality approves an application to be a collaboration coordinator, the municipality shall notify the collaboration property owners not represented by the applicant and give them the opportunity to comment. The notification shall be made in accordance with the Act on Formal Notification (2010:1932) and shall include:

  1. Information about the main content of this law.
  2. A copy of the application and the documents relied upon by the applicant.
  3. Information that the entity approved as a collaboration coordinator has the right to charge a collaboration fee from the collaboration property owners for costs as referred to in Section 1, paragraph 3, item 1 a, distributed according to Section 1, paragraph 3, item 2.
  4. A request to submit any comments within a specified period, which may not be shorter than four weeks.
  5. Information on how the collaboration property owner can follow the case and obtain the municipality's decision in the case.
  6. Information on whether simplified notification may be used in the case.
  7. Information about the provisions in this law regarding appeals of the municipality's decision on approval, as well as disputes regarding the amount and payment of the collaboration fee.

Requirements for a Municipality to Be a Collaboration Coordinator Section 5. A municipality may decide to be a collaboration coordinator only if:

  1. There is a very significant need for area-based collaboration throughout the collaboration area, and this need is not met elsewhere within the area.
  2. There is no entity that has applied to be and is eligible to be approved as a collaboration coordinator.
  3. The municipality has a collaboration plan and a collaboration budget that make it likely that a collaboration fee will be collected according to this law.
  4. The benefit factors of the collaboration properties have been determined according to this law and are reasonable.
  5. The municipality provides collaboration property owners who wish to participate in the activities with the opportunity to participate in the decision-making of the activities on reasonable and transparent terms.

Section 6. Before a municipality decides to be a collaboration coordinator pursuant to Section 5, the municipality shall notify the collaboration property owners and give them the opportunity to comment. The notification shall be made in accordance with the Act on Formal Notification (2010:1932) and shall include:

  1. Information about the main content of this law.
  2. An explanation of how the municipality has determined that there are grounds for the municipality to be a collaboration coordinator according to Section 5.
  3. Information that the collaboration coordinator has the right to charge a collaboration fee from the collaboration property owners for costs as referred to in Section 1, paragraph 3, item 1 a, distributed according to Section 1, paragraph 3, item 2.
  4. A request to submit any comments within a period of time, which may not be shorter than four weeks.
  5. Information on how the collaboration property owner can follow the case and obtain the municipality's decision in the case.
  6. Information on whether simplified notification may be used in the case.
  7. Information about the provisions in this law regarding appeals of the municipality's decision on approval, as well as disputes regarding the amount and payment of the fee.

Annual Follow-up Section 7. A collaboration coordinator shall submit an updated collaboration plan and collaboration budget to the municipality that has decided on approval as a collaboration coordinator by March 31 of each year. If the municipality is the collaboration coordinator, the municipality shall have an updated collaboration plan and collaboration budget by March 31 of each year. The collaboration coordinator shall send a copy of the plan and budget to the collaboration property owners or make the plan and budget available to the collaboration property owners on a website. If a new collaboration property owner has been added due to a change in ownership of a property or due to the addition of a new building on a collaboration property, the collaboration coordinator shall send a copy of the plan and budget to the new owner, along with information about the main content of this law and how the new owner can follow the collaboration work and participate in it.

Section 8. The municipality that has approved a collaboration coordinator shall annually review whether the conditions for approval are still met. If the municipality is the collaboration coordinator, the municipality shall annually review whether the conditions for being a collaboration coordinator are still met.

Request to Terminate a Collaboration Coordinator Status Section 9. At the request of a collaboration property owner, the municipality shall review whether the conditions for an approval according to Section 1 or a decision to be a collaboration coordinator according to Section 5 are still met.

Termination of a Collaboration Coordinator Status Section 10. If the conditions for being a collaboration coordinator are no longer met, the municipality shall decide that the coordinator status shall cease to be valid and notify the collaboration property owners of this.

Fees for the Municipality's Review Section 11. A municipality may issue regulations regarding fees for:

  1. Reviewing an application for approval according to Section 1.
  2. Annual follow-up according to Section 7.
  3. Reviewing a request according to Section 9 to terminate a coordinator status.

Appeal of Municipal Decisions Section 12. Decisions made by the municipality under this chapter may be appealed to an administrative court. A request for permission to appeal is required when appealing to the chamber court.

Chapter 3. Relationship Between a Collaboration Coordinator and a Collaboration Property Owner Collaboration Fee Section 1. A collaboration coordinator has the right to charge a collaboration fee from a collaboration property owner.

Section 2. A collaboration fee may only be charged to cover the reasonable costs incurred by the collaboration coordinator for:

  1. Conducting the collaboration work.
  2. Implementing measures that are appropriate to achieve the purpose of area-based collaboration.

The fee may not be charged for costs for which the responsibility for the costs or their distribution is regulated in other legislation, or for measures that, according to other legislation, must be implemented by a state or regional authority or by the municipality.

Section 3. The collaboration fee shall be determined so that the costs are distributed among the collaboration property owners, taking into account the benefit that the owners may reasonably be expected to receive from the costs that may be covered by the collaboration fee, according to Section 2. The benefit shall be considered proportional to the size and importance of the properties and buildings in the collaboration area, and a benefit factor shall be specified for each collaboration property.

Payment of the Collaboration Fee Section 4. A collaboration property owner shall pay the collaboration fee to the collaboration coordinator upon request.

Refund of the Collaboration Fee Section 5. If a collaboration coordinator status ends, the collaboration coordinator shall refund any collaboration fees that have been collected but have not been used.

Disputes Between a Collaboration Coordinator and a Collaboration Property Owner Section 6. Disputes between a collaboration coordinator and a collaboration property owner regarding a collaboration fee shall be decided in an ordinary court. The Code of Judicial Procedure contains provisions on the proper court in disputes.

This law shall enter into force on August 1, 2026.
tldr: The government intends to introduce a new law that would allow groups of property owners to levy a fee to improve safety and development within a specific area. The law is proposed to come into effect on August 1, 2026.
lattlast:
The government has proposed a new law on area-based collaboration. This means that groups of people or companies who own properties can come together to work on initiatives that prevent crime and make an area more attractive. The aim is to create more safety and help local businesses grow.

If such a group is approved by the municipality, they may have the right to charge a fee from the property owners who are affected by the work. Each owner pays a portion of the cost based on the benefit they receive from the work. This fee can only be used for expenses that the state or municipality does not already have a responsibility to pay.

In order to be allowed to charge fees, the group must be approved by the municipality as a "main contractor." It is also required that those leading the work represent at least two-thirds of the property owners in the area. The work must not be conducted for the purpose of making a profit.

If a dispute or disagreement arises regarding the fee between those leading the work and the property owners, the matter will be resolved in a regular court of law. The proposal is that the new law will come into effect on August 1, 2026.
